title: Intel 8086 Pin Diagram (40-Pin DIP Layout)
examTip: 8086 is a 40-pin DIP. Remember that MN/MX̄ (Pin 33) is the mode selector: connect to VCC (+5V) for Minimum Mode, or GND (0V) for Maximum Mode. This mode change alters the behavior of 8 pins (pins 24 to 31). High-order address lines (A16-A19) and BHĒ are multiplexed with status lines (S3-S6, S7) to save pin count.

children.label: Pin 33: MN/MX̄ (Mode Select)
sublabels: • Hardware strap option that determines whether
pins 24-31 act as Min or Max mode controls

theory:
The Intel 8086 is a 40-pin Dual In-line Package (DIP) 16-bit microprocessor. Due to physical pin limitations, Intel multiplexed many pins to carry different signals depending on the bus cycle state or the operational mode.

1. Multiplexed Address and Data Bus (Pins 2–16, 39):
To reduce the pin count of the package, the 8086 uses a time-multiplexed address/data bus (AD0-AD15). During the first clock cycle (T1) of a machine cycle, these pins carry the lower 16 bits of the memory or I/O address. During cycles T2, T3, Tw, and T4, they carry the 16-bit data word. The address must be latched externally during T1 using the ALE (Address Latch Enable) signal.

2. Multiplexed Address and Status Bus (Pins 35–38):
The address lines A16-A19 are multiplexed with status signals S3-S6. During T1, they carry the 4 high-order bits of the 20-bit address. During subsequent states, they carry processor status information: S3 and S4 indicate which segment register is being accessed, S5 indicates the state of the interrupt-enable flag, and S6 is always low.

3. Bus High Enable / Status S7 (Pin 34):
BHĒ is active low during T1 to indicate data transfer on the high byte of the data bus (D8-D15). It is multiplexed with status line S7, which is active during T2, T3, and T4.

4. Minimum/Maximum Mode Select (Pin 33):
The MN/MX̄ pin is a hardware configuration pin that selects the operating mode of the 8086:
- Minimum Mode (MN/MX̄ connected to +5V VCC): The CPU runs in a standalone single-processor system. It generates all bus control signals directly. The signals on pins 24 to 31 are: INTĀ, ALE, DEN̄, DT/R̄, M/IŌ, WR̄, HLDA, and HOLD.
- Maximum Mode (MN/MX̄ connected to GND): The CPU is configured for multi-processor or co-processor systems (using 8087 co-processor or 8089 I/O processor). Bus control is offloaded to the external 8288 Bus Controller. The CPU pins 24 to 31 change function to output status signals (S0̄, S1̄, S2̄), queue status signals (QS0, QS1), bus lock (LOCK̄), and request/grant signals (RQ̄/GT0̄, RQ̄/GT1̄) for multi-master bus arbitration.

5. Crucial Control & Support Signals:
- CLK (Pin 19): Clock input providing the basic timing for processor operations.
- READY (Pin 22): Input used to insert wait states for slow memory/IO devices.
- RESET (Pin 21): Input that terminates operations, clears the registers, and restarts execution from physical address FFFF0H (CS = FFFFH, IP = 0000H).
- NMI & INTR (Pins 17, 18): Interrupt inputs. NMI is non-maskable and executes Type-2 interrupt. INTR is maskable via the interrupt flag.
keyPoints: 8086 is a 40-pin DIP chip with a 20-bit Address Bus (1 MB RAM space) and 16-bit Data Bus.
MN/MX̄ (Pin 33) controls whether pins 24-31 function as Minimum Mode controllers or Maximum Mode status/arbitration lines.
Minimum Mode is for cost-effective single-processor designs, where the CPU directly generates the control bus signals.
Maximum Mode is for high-performance multi-processor designs, requiring the 8288 Bus Controller to decode S0̄, S1̄, S2̄ status lines.
Address and Data buses are multiplexed (AD0-AD15) along with high-order address and status lines (A16/S3-A19/S6, BHĒ/S7) to keep the pin count at 40.
RESET initializes the CPU to boot address FFFF0H; READY synchronizes slower devices; CLK provides fundamental timing; NMI and INTR manage interrupts.
useCases: Interfacing standard microprocessors and peripherals in a standalone system (Minimum Mode design).
Configuring multi-master microcomputer architectures with co-processors like the 8087 Math chip (Maximum Mode design).
Analyzing timing cycles, bus states (T1-T4), and physical memory latching processes in academic engineering labs.
